card.title: Why Plants Are Green
card.teaser: The sun pours out every color — so why do plants throw away the most powerful one?
card.coverImagePrompt: A single backlit leaf glowing against a golden afternoon sun, veins translucent, light rays streaming through, macro cinematic botanical photography, painterly

lessons.title: The color plants refuse
lessons.body: Sunlight is a buffet of colors, and green sits near the middle — the brightest, most energy-rich slice of what reaches Earth's surface. Yet plants, which literally eat light for a living, reject it. They bounce it back into our eyes. That's why forests look green: we're seeing the one color plants don't want.

visual.imagePrompt: A prism splitting white sunlight into a rainbow, with the green band being reflected off a leaf while other colors are absorbed

lessons.title: Meet chlorophyll
lessons.body: Inside every leaf live tiny green factories called chloroplasts, packed with a pigment called chlorophyll. Chlorophyll is tuned to absorb red and blue wavelengths — the two ends of the visible spectrum — because those wavelengths kick electrons loose in just the right way to drive photosynthesis.

keyTerms.definition: The green pigment that absorbs sunlight to power photosynthesis
The process of converting light energy into sugar using CO₂ and water

visual.imagePrompt: A microscopic view inside a plant cell showing chloroplasts packed with green chlorophyll molecules

lessons.title: Why skip the brightest light?
lessons.body: It seems wasteful — green light is the strongest wavelength coming from the sun. But biologists now think early photosynthetic bacteria evolved under purple, sulfur-eating microbes that hogged the green part of the spectrum. The plants' ancestors had to make do with what was left: red and blue. The color of life on Earth is essentially a 3-billion-year-old compromise.
lessons.funFact: If purple bacteria had gone extinct first, we might be living on a planet of purple forests instead.

visual.imagePrompt: An ancient ocean scene with purple microbial mats covering the seafloor under early sunlight, geological cross-section

lessons.title: A stability story, not a power story
lessons.body: A 2020 study in Science argued another reason: absorbing green would actually be unstable. Leaves need steady energy input, not maximum input. By reflecting the noisy, variable green band, plants keep photosynthesis smooth — like a car that deliberately ignores sudden gusts of wind to cruise safely.

visual.imagePrompt: A scientific graph showing red and blue light absorption peaks in chlorophyll with green as a reflected valley

lessons.title: A planet painted by limitation
lessons.body: Every spring meadow, every forest canopy, every houseplant on your windowsill is the color it is because of what it doesn't use. Green isn't a flag of abundance — it's the echo of an evolutionary trade-off older than animals, older than fish, older than bones.

quiz.question: Which wavelengths of light does chlorophyll absorb most strongly?
quiz.explanation: Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue wavelengths and reflects green — which is why leaves appear green.

quiz.question: According to one theory, why didn't early plants evolve to absorb green light?
options: Purple bacteria were already using the green band
Green light doesn't reach Earth's surface
Green light is too weak to matter
quiz.explanation: Early photosynthetic organisms like purple bacteria absorbed green light, leaving the ancestors of plants to adapt to red and blue.

quiz.question: What benefit does reflecting green light give plants, according to the 2020 Science study?
quiz.explanation: Rejecting the brightest, noisiest slice of light helps plants maintain a smooth, reliable flow of energy.

sources.title: Quieting a noisy antenna reproduces photosynthetic light-harvesting spectra
